segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a man in a blue suit, white shirt, and blue-rimmed glasses speaking directly to the camera with a serious, informative tone, introducing "the deadliest infectious disease of all time." He narrates its 3-million-year history, predating Homo sapiens, and its presence in ancient Egyptian mummies and the Hebrew Bible. As he continues, the visuals transition to abstract, symmetrical, kaleidoscopic patterns resembling expanding and contracting ink blots or cellular structures, with dark, organic textures in sepia, black, and muted reds, creating a somber, decaying aesthetic. The man reappears intermittently, maintaining his composed demeanor, while his narration details how medical advances like vaccines and antibiotics have saved millions, yet in 2022, this disease killed more people than malaria, typhoid, cholera, homicide, and war combined. Over these evolving, fractal-like designs, text overlays appear within dark, circular ink-like frames, displaying words such as 'huaifu [destroyed palace]' and 'schachepeth [wasting away]'. The speaker articulates these as ancient names for the disease, explaining "huaifu" as "destroyed palace" from ancient China, "shakafeth" as "wasting away" from ancient Hebrew, and the 19th-century term "consumption" for how it seemed to "consume the body." The abstract visuals, with their microscopic, decaying motifs, visually reinforce the speaker's narrative of a pervasive, destructive illness.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of the Man in Blue Suit, his blue-rimmed glasses and graying hair prominent, as he speaks with a serious, calm, and authoritative voice, stating, "Today, we call it tuberculosis." The scene then transitions to abstract kaleidoscopic patterns of symmetrical, ink-blot-like designs in sepia and dark tones, evolving into fractal, mirrored textures resembling cellular decay. Over this, elegant serif text appears in dark, circular frames, displaying phrases like 'huaifu [destroyed palace]' and 'schachepeth [wasting away]', all underscored by a low, sustained, slightly ominous ambient hum. A title card then appears, boldly proclaiming 'THE DEADLIEST INFECTIOUS DISEASE OF ALL TIME' in red serif font, followed by a 'CC' logo within a red apple shape and the text 'A CRASH COURSE LECTURE'. The Man in Blue Suit reappears, now standing behind a wooden desk in a vintage classroom setting with a chalkboard displaying dated medical notes from 1909. He gestures expressively as he continues his lecture, his voice maintaining an educational and somber tone, explaining that tuberculosis "reveals fundamental truths about who we are as human beings" and questioning why, despite human advancements and identifying a cure, "so many people are still dying of it." A subtle, almost mechanical whirring sound accompanies a cut to a split-screen montage of sepia-toned historical medical photographs: a doctor examining a patient in 1933, a nurse attending to a patient in bed, and a sign for 'HOSPITAL DEPENDARE'. Text overlays highlight the lethality of infectious diseases, stating '90% of people die of disease' and then '90% of natural causes'. The Man in Blue Suit returns, resuming his lecture with animated hand gestures, emphasizing that "90% of people die of disease, a phenomenon so entrenched in human life that we describe many such deaths as natural causes."

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with the Man in Blue Suit, a man with short, graying hair, wearing a blue suit and glasses, standing in a dimly lit Vintage Classroom Setting. He lectures with expressive hand gestures in front of a chalkboard displaying historical medical notes from January 26, 1909, including 'Pathology of Gross Lesions' and 'Toxic Insanities: alcohol and various drugs.' He speaks articulately, noting that history often focuses on "wars and empires and trade routes" rather than "microbes." The scene transitions to a split-screen montage of sepia-toned historical medical photographs, showing a doctor examining a patient, a nurse attending to a patient, and a 'HOSPITAL DEPENDARE' sign, overlaid with text stating '90% of people die of disease' and '90% of natural causes,' visually reinforcing the historical impact of infectious diseases. The lecturer then quotes Virginia Woolf, whose sepia-toned portrait appears alongside her observation that "(it is) 'strange indeed that illness has not taken its place with love, battle, and jealousy among the prime themes of literature.'" He adds, "Hamlet never gets a headache, but the rest of us do." A sequence of diverse historical artworks follows, including a medieval engraving of a knight and woman, a mosaic of Alexander the Great on horseback, a colorful manuscript illustration of a royal banquet, a red-toned sketch of a man being examined, and a dramatic painting of a dying figure surrounded by mourners. During this montage, the lecturer discusses the human tendency to imagine we choose our fate and that history is a story of human choice, referencing the rumor that Alexander the Great died of poisoning, despite likely succumbing to typhoid or malaria. He emphasizes that "we simply don't want a world where even the world's most successful emperor can be felled by mere infection." The Man in Blue Suit reappears behind a wooden desk in the same classroom, continuing his animated lecture, concluding that "yet that is the world we live." The segment ends with a brief, blurred transition back to the academic setting, maintaining a consistent historical and medical theme throughout.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a sepia-toned historical montage of medical scenes, including a doctor examining a patient with a stethoscope and a wide shot of a hospital ward, emphasizing the institutional treatment of illness in the past. A male voice, belonging to the Man in Blue Suit, begins to speak, describing "a world where disease shapes history in profound ways." This transitions into a split-screen format, showing a vintage hospital building on the left and a nurse attending to a child in a crib on the right, as the Man in Blue Suit continues, explaining how "history shapes disease" and that while "anyone can get tuberculosis... not everyone has an equal chance of getting it, and certainly not everyone has an equal chance of dying from it." The split-screen then displays an X-ray of a human chest on the left and medical staff observing a patient on the right. The scene shifts to a close-up of the Man in Blue Suit, who speaks directly to the camera with a serious expression, his blue-rimmed glasses and graying hair visible against a dark, textured background, stating, "To understand why, I had to go back, way back, to discover how our earliest understandings of TB evolved alongside culture into the problem we're now facing." The video then cuts to a title card with the bold red text 'TUBERCULOSIS IS WEIRD' over an abstract, kaleidoscopic pattern resembling cellular decay, accompanied by a low, ominous, slightly distorted musical tone. The Man in Blue Suit reappears, now standing behind a wooden desk in a vintage classroom setting filled with old medical equipment, gesturing expressively as he lectures, saying, "First, let me give you a very basic overview of our current understanding of the disease, which can be summarized as: Tuberculosis is weird." He continues to explain that "It's an infectious disease, meaning it's caused by germs, like COVID-19, it's spread through the air we breathe," as the video concludes with a grid of white human silhouettes on a dark background, which gradually turn red, symbolizing the spread of infection, while he reveals that "around a quarter of all humans are currently infected with Mycobacterium tuberculosis."

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a grid of white human silhouettes on a dark brown background, gradually turning red as a black circular mark spreads from the chest of one figure, visually symbolizing the spread of disease. The camera then zooms in on a single red silhouette with a dark, expanding circle in the chest, visually representing infection. A male speaker, the Man in Blue Suit, is heard explaining that "only about 5 to 10% of them will ever progress to what is called active disease, which is when the infection makes you chronically sick." He is then seen standing behind a wooden desk in a vintage classroom setting, speaking directly to the camera with expressive hand gestures, continuing to elaborate that if an infection proceeds to active disease, it usually happens within two years, but "TB also may lie dormant for decades before suddenly exploding." The background features shelves with glass bottles, a microscope, and a clock, reinforcing the historical medical theme. The scene transitions to a split-screen montage of vintage, color-tinted footage: on the left, a child with crutches walks with an adult, and on the right, a military tank moves through a crowd. This is followed by black-and-white archival footage of a scientist working in a lab, handling test tubes and equipment. The speaker continues, asking "What causes this progression to active disease? Well, again, it's complicated. We don't fully know, but risk factors include malnutrition, lack of access to medical care, crowded housing conditions, and a severely compromised immune system, especially from insufficiently treated HIV infection." The narrative shifts to an animated anatomical sequence showing a human figure with visible lungs and a dark, spreading infection in the chest, transitioning to a patient lying in bed with a nightstand and medical supplies. The speaker adds, "But even once the disease becomes active, its course is extremely unpredictable. It may kill its victims within a few weeks or over many years. It usually attacks the lungs, but can also invade other organs like the brain or pancreas or spinal column." The scene then displays a triptych of medical imagery: a labeled anatomical drawing of the brain, a close-up of a diseased organ with visible blood vessels, and a detailed illustration of the pancreas and liver, visually aligning with the speaker's description of affected organs. The video returns to the Man in Blue Suit, who continues his lecture with animated gestures, emphasizing his points, concluding with "For reasons we don't fully understand..." The overall tone is educational and historical, blending animation, archival footage, and live-action to convey the impact and progression of disease.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with the Man in Blue Suit, a male speaker with a clear, articulate voice, lecturing directly to the camera from a vintage laboratory setting. He explains that while some patients recover from tuberculosis without treatment, most untreated individuals will eventually die from the disease. He delves into the strangeness of the bacterium, *Mycobacterium tuberculosis*, detailing its unusually fatty, thick cell wall that makes it difficult for infection-fighting cells to destroy. As he describes the bacterium being surrounded, a stylized, sepia-toned 'Tubercle Animation' appears, visually depicting a glowing rod-shaped bacterium being enveloped by translucent circular structures that multiply to form a flower-like pattern, with the word 'Tubercle' appearing in bold white text. The speaker clarifies that this calcifying tissue is called a tubercle, and as long as these spherical tubercles hold the bacteria, active disease doesn't develop. However, he warns that if the bacteria escape and the immune system is overwhelmed, the body can be overcome by infection, leading to death. This is visually reinforced by a 'Grid of Human Silhouettes' appearing, showing white outlines of diverse individuals gradually increasing in number and turning red, symbolizing the spread of infection within a population. The scene then returns to the Man in Blue Suit, who continues his animated lecture, emphasizing the bacterium's slow process of building its cell wall.

This bacterium is gradually enveloped by translucent, circular structures that multiply and cluster around it, forming a flower-like pattern. The word 'Tubercle' appears in bold, white serif text above the central structure, identifying the formation. The animation continues to show multiple such tubercles spreading across the screen. The scene then shifts to a series of white human silhouettes appearing one by one, forming a growing crowd of diverse individuals—men, women, and children—drawn in a simple, chalk-like outline style. The silhouettes are static but increase in number, suggesting a population. The video returns to the Man in Blue Suit, who continues his lecture with animated gestures, reinforcing the educational tone of the segment. The overall narrative visually explains the biological process of tuberculosis formation and its spread within a population, using a blend of live-action and animated sequences.

audio_updated_entities.Grid of Human Silhouettes: A dark background filled with rows of white human outlines, which gradually turn red and increase in number, visually representing the spread of infection and the overwhelming of a population, directly illustrating the speaker's explanation of disease progression and eventual death.
audio_updated_entities.Tubercle Animation: A stylized, sepia-toned animation showing a rod-shaped bacterium being enveloped by translucent circular structures that multiply and form a flower-like pattern, with the word 'Tubercle' appearing in bold white text above the central formation. This animation directly illustrates the biological process of tuberculosis formation as described by the speaker, who explicitly names and details the 'tubercle' formation.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with the Man in Blue Suit, a man with short, graying hair and blue-rimmed glasses, seated in a vintage laboratory setting, speaking directly to the camera with expressive hand gestures. He explains in a clear, lecturing male voice that Mycobacterium tuberculosis has an "incredibly slow growth rate" compared to other bacteria like E. coli, dividing "about once per day" versus E. coli's "once every 20 minutes," which means infections "simply take longer to make a person sick." This explanation is visually reinforced by a split-screen animation showing the slow multiplication of rod-shaped 'Mycobacterium tuberculosis' on the left and the rapid, dense growth of 'E. coli' on the right. The Man in Blue Suit continues, clarifying that "TB is not like a plague" that sweeps through a community, nor is it like "a cancer or heart disease" that rarely spreads, or an "E. coli infection where you get violently sick all at once." During his lecture, a montage of grainy, vintage archival footage appears, showing a woman in traditional attire holding a child, a medical worker at a desk with patients, a chart listing 'PRINCIPAL CAUSES OF DEATH IN THE UNITED STATES', and a nurse attending to a patient. He concludes by stating that tuberculosis's fundamental difference from other infections is "precisely why the classical understandings... varied so widely," and why "even today, the stories we tell ourselves about the disease reveal at least as much about humans as they do about tuberculosis." The video then cuts to a dark, textured background with bold red text, stating 'MAN GOT TO TELL HIMSELF HE UNDERSTAND', suggesting a philosophical conclusion.
segments.visual_original_caption: The video opens with the Man in Blue Suit seated in a vintage laboratory setting, speaking directly to the camera with expressive hand gestures, surrounded by wooden shelves filled with glass bottles and scientific equipment. The scene transitions to a split-screen animation showing the multiplication of rod-shaped bacteria, labeled 'E. coli' on the right and 'Mycobacterium tuberculosis' on the left, emphasizing their differing growth rates. This is followed by a montage of grainy, vintage archival footage: a woman in traditional attire holding a child, a medical worker at a desk with patients, and a chart listing 'PRINCIPAL CAUSES OF DEATH IN THE UNITED STATES' including heart disease, cancer, and nephritis, alongside a nurse attending to a patient. The video then returns to the Man in Blue Suit, who continues his lecture with animated gestures, before cutting to a dark, textured background with bold red text stating 'MAN GOT TO TELL HIMSELF HE UNDERSTAND', suggesting a philosophical or educational conclusion to the segment.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a Man in Blue Suit, speaking with a clear, authoritative male voice, lecturing from a dimly lit Vintage Classroom Setting. He gestures expressively as he explains that in antiquity, tuberculosis was understood in "many different ways," with some believing it genetic, others contagious, or caused by "foul air" or "disharmony of fluids within the body." Behind him, a chalkboard displays medical notes from January 26, 1909, including 'Toxic Insanities' and 'Pathology of Gross Lesions.' Gentle, slightly melancholic background music plays throughout. The scene transitions to a split-screen montage of Archival Lab Footage, showing a scientist with a microscope, a car, and a 'NO SWIMMING WATER POLLUTED' sign, followed by a woman drinking from a glass. The Man in Blue Suit's voice continues over these visuals, noting that some even "approached a germ theory of disease" hundreds of years before the microscope. This leads to a colorful Historical Artwork Montage, showing a medieval illustration of a man examining a patient. Next, a dark screen features a portrait of Ibn Sina and a Text Overlay quoting him: 'tainted foreign organisms that are not visible by naked eye.' The Man in Blue Suit attributes this quote to Ibn Sina, highlighting its impressiveness despite the lack of pre-microscopy confirmation. The video cuts back to the Man in Blue Suit in a close-up, his blue-rimmed glasses and graying hair clearly visible, as he concludes by emphasizing humanity's desire to understand "why things happen, especially why really bad things happen."

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of the Man in Blue Suit, a man with graying hair and blue-rimmed glasses, speaking directly to the camera with a serious expression, stating, "immediately apparent, we will find one." The scene transitions to a wider shot, revealing him standing behind a wooden desk in a dimly lit Vintage Classroom Setting, where a chalkboard displays medical notes from January 26, 1909, including 'Pathology of Gross Lesions' and 'Toxic Insanities: alcohol and various drugs.' He gestures expressively as he lectures, introducing a short poem by Kurt Vonnegut. As he recites, "Tiger got to hunt, bird got to fly; Man got to sit and wonder why, why, why?", a sepia-toned, vintage-style grid of illustrations featuring a tiger, a bird, and a historical portrait appears, accompanied by the evolving text of the poem. He continues, "Tiger got to sleep, bird got to land, Man got to tell himself he understand," as the text on screen changes to 'Man got to tell himself he understand,' with the final phrase underlined in red. The Man in Blue Suit reappears, explaining that this brings them to "an important facet of understanding human responses to illness," before the word 'stigma' is displayed in elegant script below him, which he pronounces with emphasis. A brief mechanical whirring sound accompanies a scene shift to sepia-toned photographs: first, a man in a plaid shirt with a young boy in a scout uniform, followed by a family portrait of four people—two adults and two boys—dressed formally, smiling outdoors. The Man in Blue Suit continues to speak, explaining that in their desire to answer "why, why, why" by telling themselves they understand, "humans commonly construct moral and ethical narratives around illness." He shares a personal anecdote, stating, "my dad had cancer twice when I was a kid, and I saw some of this firsthand. People kept their distance from us. Some said he got cancer because his parents smoked, or because he didn't exercise enough, or because he didn't eat broccoli or whatever," highlighting the judgmental narratives people create. The lighting throughout is warm and nostalgic, emphasizing the historical and reflective tone of the narrative.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with the Man in Blue Suit, a man with short, graying hair and blue-rimmed glasses, standing behind a wooden desk in a dimly lit Vintage Classroom Setting. He gestures expressively as he delivers a clear, articulate lecture, discussing how second-hand smoke and poor diet are risk factors for cancer, but noting that most people whose parents smoked do not get cancer, referencing a '32-year-old father of two young kids.' The chalkboard behind him displays medical notes from January 26, 1909, including 'Pathology of Gross Lesions' and 'Toxic Insanities.' As he continues his lecture, the scene transitions to sepia-toned Family Photographs, first showing a man in a plaid shirt with a young boy in a scout uniform, then two young boys in swimwear jumping joyfully in a wooded area, evoking a nostalgic, personal history that aligns with his mention of family. The video cuts back to the Man in Blue Suit in a close-up, speaking with a serious expression about not wanting to reckon with a world that is merely unfair, where people get sick not because they did something wrong, but because the world is unjust or random. He then returns to the full classroom view, continuing his animated lecture, explaining that we often blame the sufferer by creating explanations, defining stigma as saying 'you deserved this to happen' while implying 'I don't deserve it.' The sequence ends with a final Family Photograph of a man in a plaid shirt and a young boy in a scout uniform, reinforcing the theme of personal memory and familial connection, as the speaker concludes that stigma can become a double burden for the sick.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with the Man in Blue Suit, a man with short, graying hair and blue-rimmed glasses, standing behind a wooden desk in a dimly lit vintage classroom. He speaks directly to the camera with expressive hand gestures, delivering a serious lecture. Behind him, a large chalkboard displays medical notes from January 26, 1909, detailing 'Toxic Insanities: alcohol and various drugs' and 'Pathology of Gross Lesions,' including conditions like anemia and syphilis. The speaker's clear, articulate voice discusses the societal challenge of having one's humanity discounted due to illness, citing how tuberculosis patients in the 18th and 19th centuries were called 'invalids.' As he utters the word, an elegant serif text overlay of 'invalids' appears on screen. He continues, explaining that fighting the stigma of illness was often harder than fighting the disease itself. The scene then transitions to a split-screen montage while the speaker elaborates on the complex nature of stigma and hallmarks of highly stigmatized illnesses, such as chronic or infectious diseases. On the left, black-and-white archival footage shows signs for 'WEBSTER ON PESTILENCE,' 'NATIONAL FOUNDATION FOR INFANTILE PARALYSIS,' and 'NATIONAL MULTIPLE SCLEROSIS SOCIETY.' On the right, colorized vintage footage depicts a domestic scene with a man in a military uniform holding a baby and a woman knitting. The video returns to the Man in Blue Suit, who, with animated gestures, concludes his point by stating that the perceived origin of a disease, especially if linked to personal choice, also significantly impacts stigma.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens in a dimly lit, vintage classroom where a Man in Blue Suit, wearing blue-rimmed glasses, lectures expressively behind a wooden desk. Behind him, a chalkboard displays medical notes from January 26, 1909, detailing 'Toxic Insanities' and 'Pathology of Gross Lesions.' He speaks with a serious, authoritative tone, explaining how illnesses like major depression and substance abuse are often stigmatized, with people told to "just choose to be happier" or "just choose to quit drinking." He adds that "some cancers and heart diseases are stigmatized for resulting from purported choice as well," emphasizing that "this is not how biology works. Illness has no moral compass. It does not punish the evil and reward the good." He continues, stating, "we want life to be a story that makes sense," which led to beliefs in the mid-20th century that "cancer was caused by things like social isolation." This coincides with a split-screen montage: on the left, a black-and-white photograph of two babies with a bottle, and on the right, newspaper headlines like 'Loneliness kills' and 'SOCIAL ISOLATION MAY FACTOR IN CANCER DEATHS.' The Man in Blue Suit then recounts how "parents were actually told their kids got leukemia because they hadn't been adequately loved as infants." Returning to his lecture, he asserts, "If a clear cause and effect isn't present, we will invent one, even if it's cruel." The segment concludes with a split-screen, showing the Man in Blue Suit on the right, while on the left, a grid of sepia-toned illustrations (a tiger, a bird, and a historical portrait) appears with evolving text: 'Tiger got to hunt, bird got to fly; Man got to sit and wonder why, why, why?' and then 'Man got to tell himself he understand,' underlined in red, as the Man in Blue Suit's audio partially states, "Because tigers got to sleep and birds got to..."

He stands behind a wooden desk in a vintage classroom setting, gesturing expressively as he delivers a lecture on the stigmatization of illnesses like depression, substance abuse, cancer, and heart disease, arguing that biology has no moral compass and that humans often invent causes for diseases, even cruel ones, when a clear cause and effect is absent. His voice is clear and authoritative, explaining historical beliefs about social isolation causing cancer and lack of love causing leukemia in infants, and partially quoting a philosophical reflection about tigers and birds.
audio_updated_entities.Sepia-Toned Illustration Grid: A vintage-style grid of sepia-toned illustrations featuring a tiger, a bird, and a historical portrait, accompanied by evolving text that reads 'Tiger got to hunt, bird got to fly; Man got to sit and wonder why, why, why?' and later 'Man got to tell himself he understand,' with the final phrase underlined in red. This visual appears on a split-screen, directly correlating with the Man in Blue Suit's audio as he partially states, 'Because tigers got to sleep and birds got to...'
audio_updated_entities.Family Photographs: A black-and-white photograph of two babies interacting with a bottle, appearing on a split-screen montage alongside newspaper headlines, visually illustrating the Man in Blue Suit's discussion about the historical belief that infants could get leukemia due to inadequate love.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a split-screen: on the left, a sepia-toned grid displays three illustrations—a tiger, a bird, and a historical portrait—with evolving text that reads, 'Man got to tell himself he understand,' a phrase spoken by the Man in Blue Suit on the right. He is seen speaking directly to the camera in a vintage classroom setting, gesturing expressively. He begins to lecture, stating that 'TB occupies an interesting place in this conversation,' describing it as a chronic disease with high levels of perceived peril that wasn't always understood as infectious or a choice, and was often associated with 'demon possession or excessive alcohol consumption or moral frailty.' The scene transitions to a full view of the Man in Blue Suit standing before a chalkboard with medical notes from 1909, including 'Pathology of Gross Lesions' and 'Toxic Insanities: alcohol and various drugs,' as he continues his lecture. The video then cuts to a series of historical illustrations: a young girl in bed with a spectral figure above her, a sketch of two men at a table with wine glasses, and a portrait of Gideon Harvey with a skeleton and alchemist in the background. The Harvey portrait is shown alongside a vintage advertisement for 'Swift's Specific,' promoting it as a cure for blood poisons with testimonials. The Man in Blue Suit reappears, speaking with intensity, explaining that in Northern Europe, it became 'untenable to understand the disease then known as consumption through only a stigmatic lens' because 'the disease had exploded,' making stigmatization harder 'when everyone seems to be getting it, not just the poor or the vulnerable, but even the rich,' noting that 'the richest individual of the 19th century died...' The video then shifts to a sepia-toned photograph of a doctor examining a patient in bed, followed by a black-and-white image of a hospital ward with multiple patients. The final sequence shows a woman reading beside a sleeping child in a garden, then a split-screen with a portrait of Jay Gould (1836–1892) flanked by abstract, symmetrical patterns resembling spinal vertebrae and a textured geometric design, suggesting a thematic link between historical figures, illness, and biological structure. A subtle, historical-sounding background music track accompanies the visuals and narration throughout.

segments.integrated_caption: The minute opens with a split-screen montage of sepia-toned historical portraits, featuring James Monroe, Andrew Jackson, and Eliza Poe, as a male voice begins to speak, noting that TB killed "presidents and kings and poets and actresses." The scene then transitions to a surreal, vintage-style black-and-white image of a woman in a dress emerging from a large clamshell on a rocky shore, with the shell opening to reveal her lying inside. This visual is accompanied by a portrait of Henry David Thoreau, whose quote about death and decay being beautiful, "like the pearly tear of the shellfish or the hectic glow of consumption," is read aloud by the same male voice, explaining how consumption became a "romanticized disease, a disease of beauty and refinement and intellectual sophistication." The video then shifts to a modern-day lecture setting, where the Man in Blue Suit stands behind a wooden desk in a vintage classroom, gesturing expressively as he continues his lecture. The chalkboard behind him displays medical notes from January 26, 1909, detailing 'Toxic Insanities: alcohol and various drugs' and 'Pathology of Gross Lesions,' including anemia, hemorrhage, brain tumor, and syphilis. The lighting is dim and academic, with vintage medical equipment visible on the desk. The Man in Blue Suit discusses the temptation to romanticize disease versus stigmatizing it, sharing his personal understanding "first hand that the two aren't entirely separable" as someone who "suffers from mental illness," explaining how both strategies "ultimately other the sufferer." The video concludes with a brief, ghostly, monochrome image of the Man in Blue Suit’s face superimposed over abstract, swirling patterns, as his lecture continues, cutting off mid-sentence while discussing how mental illness is often romanticized.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with grainy, black-and-white archival footage of a scientist in a lab coat handling equipment in a dimly lit laboratory, suggesting early 20th-century medical research. This transitions to a close-up of a child’s wide, expressive eyes, evoking vulnerability, as a male voice questions, "on creative genius or other superpowers?" followed by a brief, unsettling, distorted sound. The scene then cuts to the Man in Blue Suit, a male lecturer with a clear, articulate voice, standing behind a wooden desk in a vintage classroom. He gestures expressively as he explains that "mental illness is also heavily stigmatized in our culture," and how "romanticization and stigmatization are essentially like complementary strategies for casting people out of society and finding a story that makes sense for why those people ended up sick." Behind him, a a chalkboard displays medical notes from January 26, 1909, detailing 'Toxic Insanities' and 'Pathology of Gross Lesions,' with listed conditions like 'Anemia,' 'Hemorrhage,' 'Brain Tumor,' and 'Syphilis,' and the names 'Professor Sternk' and 'Dr. Jackson' visible. A split-screen montage of vintage medical footage—showing nurses with patients in hospital beds and a wide ward scene—appears as the lecturer's voice continues, explaining that "In the case of consumption, this romanticization goes deep and wide," and how its impact on contemporary culture makes one "wonder if there's anything in our lives today that's not connected to TB." The Man in Blue Suit then reappears, continuing his animated lecture. A title card with 'THE ALLURE OF CONSUMPTION' in bold, golden serif font against a dark, textured background appears, accompanied by a low, melancholic, string-based orchestral score. The lecturer reappears, continuing his discourse over the soft, melancholic music, stating that "classically, consumption was viewed as a disease of the air. It was an illness of the breath, of the place where the body interacts with the atmosphere." The scene transitions to a final title card displaying 'human soul:' in elegant white script over a swirling, abstract, sepia-toned background resembling organic matter or cellular structures, as the lecturer begins to say, "Consider many of our words for the human..." with the melancholic music still playing.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with abstract, kaleidoscopic patterns in sepia and dark brown, displaying the elegant white text 'human soul:'. A male speaker immediately begins explaining how words for 'soul' in different languages – Hebrew 'ruach', Chinese 'chi', English 'spirit', and Inuit 'sila' – all derive from words meaning 'breath' or 'breathing', with each word appearing sequentially in square frames with ink-blot designs as he names them. The background then shifts to a faint X-ray of a human chest, illustrating his point as the words 'breath', 'inspired' (to breathe in), and 'expired' (to breathe all the way out) appear, emphasizing the linguistic and biological connection between breath and life. The scene transitions to the Man in Blue Suit, who is the speaker, standing behind a wooden desk in a vintage classroom. A chalkboard behind him lists medical topics from 1909, including 'Toxic Insanities' and 'Pathology of Gross Lesions'. He gestures expressively as he continues to lecture, explaining how it was easy to link this 'disease of breath' (referring to consumption) to a 'disease of the spirit, the chi, the sila, and the ruach', noting that consumptives were often seen as 'paragons of brilliance and beauty'. The video then cuts to a montage of historical artworks, including a Pre-Raphaelite-style painting of a woman, a sepia photograph of a man in a study, and a triptych of three sepia-toned portraits of historical figures. During this visual sequence, the speaker elaborates that in men, consumption was even believed to bring 'creative powers to new levels', citing examples like Stephen Crane, Frederick Chopin, and the sculptor of the Statue of Liberty, who all died of consumption. The Man in Blue Suit reappears in a close-up, his blue-rimmed glasses and graying hair visible, speaking directly to the camera with a serious expression, concluding by pointing out that less attention was paid to the fact that 'in a world where roughly 30% of all Northern Europeans were dying of a disease'.

The scene transitions to a split-screen, where a beige card displays Lord Byron's quote, 'I should like, I think, to die of consumption... because then the women would all say, ‘See that poor Byron – how interesting he looks in dying!’', which the lecturer reads aloud. On the right, a sepia-toned portrait of Lord Byron in profile, hand on chin, is visible. A subtle, ethereal sound effect plays briefly before the video cuts back to the Man in Blue Suit, now standing behind a wooden desk in a vintage classroom setting, gesturing expressively. The chalkboard behind him clearly displays medical notes from 1909, including 'Pathology of Gross Lesions' and 'Toxic Insanities'. He continues his lecture, explaining how consumption was believed to grant men artistic heights and make women more beautiful and ethereal. The scene shifts to a triptych of historical artworks featuring women, followed by a left panel displaying Charlotte Brontë's quote, 'Consumption, I am aware, is a flattering malady,' which the lecturer also reads. He then reveals that this 'flattering malady' would kill Charlotte and all three of her sisters. After a brief return to the Man in Blue Suit, the video shows a series of historical artworks: a young girl in a red dress beside a bedridden figure, then a watercolor of a woman in a green dress gazing out a window at a pastoral landscape, and finally a close-up of her serene face. Throughout these visuals, the lecturer describes how patients with active TB typically become pale and thin, with rosy cheeks and wide, sunken eyes due to low blood oxygen.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of a watercolor painting depicting a young girl in a red dress beside a bedridden figure in a dimly lit room, evoking themes of illness. The scene then shifts to the Man in Blue Suit, who, speaking directly to the camera from a vintage classroom with a chalkboard displaying 1909 medical notes like 'Pathology of Gross Lesions' and 'Toxic Insanities,' explains the "degeneration and fevers that accompany the disease" of consumption. He introduces the English actor Eliza Poe, whose sepia-toned portrait appears, noting her widely admired beauty was "stereotypically tubercular," characterized by "rosy cheeks, alabaster skin, wide eyes and tiny body," all results of consumption which killed her in 1811 at age 23 or 24. The speaker adds that her son, Edgar Allan Poe, whose vintage engraved portrait is then shown, was two at the time and later described women in his stories as similarly "wispy, pale, and large-eyed." As the Man in Blue Suit gestures emphatically, the phrase 'consumptive chic' appears on a red banner, and he elaborates that this aesthetic had "taken over European beauty standards" by Eliza's death. A split-screen montage of vintage advertisements, including one for A.L. Moody’s Corset Dept. and a dandruff remedy, then an ad for 'BRIGHT-EYES GOOD-BYE' (depicting a woman with Belladonna) alongside a painting of a girl with cherries, visually illustrates the speaker's explanation. He describes how American and European magazines encouraged women to apply "the poison Belladonna to their eyelids to dilate their pupils" for that "wide-eyed consumptive look," and offered instructions for applying "red paint to the lips and cheeks." The segment concludes with a vintage trade card for 'ENAMEL AMERICA' featuring a woman holding an American flag, promoting complexion beautification, followed by a close-up of her pale, large-eyed face, reinforcing the historical idealization of this 'consumptive chic' beauty standard.

segments.integrated_caption: The minute opens with a split-screen montage of fashion imagery, showing models in a dark, structured blue dress and a black lace dress, as the audio continues discussing 'the hectic glow of consumptive fevers.' The scene then transitions to a triptych of vintage advertisements, including a 1950s Pepsi-Cola ad with the slogan 'Fashion is for the Slender,' a Calvin Klein 'OBSESSION' ad, and a 'HEALTHY WEEKLY FOR WOMEN' magazine cover. Over these visuals, the Man in Blue Suit, speaking in a clear, lecturing tone, explains that 'from runway shows to fashion magazines, these standards of beauty are still molding what is considered to be feminine beauty.' He then appears behind a wooden desk in a vintage classroom, gesturing as he speaks, with a chalkboard displaying medical notes from 1909 about 'Toxic Insanities' and 'Pathology of Gross Lesions.' He states that 'TB shaped not only the makeup and beauty standards of the era, but also fashion, which for women, sought to emphasize the visible symptoms of illness.' This is followed by a split-screen of historical illustrations, one of a Victorian woman and another of a woman with a skeleton. The video then shows a triptych of corset advertisements, featuring tightly laced corsets, as the Man in Blue Suit describes 'the most glaring such clothing was the pointed corset, which emphasized the narrowness of women's waists.' He continues, explaining that 'the similarity, by the way, between waste and waist, is an etymological coincidence in English, but a telling one,' as text overlays for 'waste' and 'waist' appear on a dark background, with him in a small inset. A black-and-white close-up of a corset being laced tightly illustrates his point that 'these corsets were so restrictive that women were limited in the kinds of physical activities they could accomplish and struggled to get full breaths of air.' A vintage photograph of a woman lying ill in bed with a nurse attending to her appears as he adds, 'Both of which mirrored the experience of actual consumption.' The Man in Blue Suit returns, concluding that 'consumption had become fully glamorous and idealized.' The minute ends with a split-screen showing a sepia-toned portrait of a bearded man and a beige card with a quote attributed to Charles Dickens, which the Man in Blue Suit begins to recite: 'There is a dread disease...'

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a sepia-toned portrait of a bearded man in profile, accompanied by a beige card displaying a quote attributed to Charles Dickens, which a male speaker immediately recites: "There is a dread disease, in which the struggle between soul and body is so gradual, quiet, and solemn... that the spirit grows light and sanguine with its lightening load." The scene transitions to a vintage classroom where the Man in Blue Suit, wearing blue-rimmed glasses, lectures expressively behind a wooden desk. Behind him, a chalkboard displays medical notes from January 26, 1909, including 'Toxic Insanities' and 'Pathology of Gross Lesions.' He explains that Dickens didn't name the disease because "Everyone knew of the disease that shrank the body and enlarged the soul." The speaker then states, "this all belies the true violence of consumption," as the video cuts to a classical portrait of the English poet John Keats, labeled with his name, whom the speaker identifies as "the most famous 19th-century victim of tuberculosis." Black-and-white archival footage of the 'Krankenhaus der Stadt Wien' and medical professionals in white coats is shown as the speaker recounts Keats's autopsy, where "the lungs were completely gone." The Man in Blue Suit reappears, continuing his intense lecture, explaining the grim reality of death by tuberculosis: "The afflicted often drown as blood and pus fills their lungs. segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a sepia-toned, textured background featuring symmetrical, ink-blot-like patterns, setting a somber, historical tone. The title 'THE WHITE MAN'S PLAGUE' appears in bold, red serif font against a dark, grungy backdrop, accompanied by eerie, low, sustained string music. The scene transitions to the Man in Blue Suit, who stands behind a wooden desk in a vintage classroom setting, speaking directly to the camera with a serious, authoritative expression. He begins, "Here is where the romanticization of tuberculosis becomes, believe it or not, even more insidious," explaining that consumption was seen as "a disease of civilization." Behind him, a chalkboard displays medical notes from 1909, reinforcing the historical and academic context. The video then cuts to a split-screen montage of black-and-white archival footage, illustrating the historical context: a classroom scene, a nurse with patients in a hospital, a scientist working with test tubes, and a close-up of a handwritten hospital record. The Man in Blue Suit continues, detailing how "city dwellers were far more likely to develop consumption" and how this led to a "racialization of the illness." He reappears, now with a text overlay quoting 'a disease of the master race not of the slave race,' visually emphasizing the racial undertones of the historical medical narrative. He elaborates that "most white doctors believed that TB could only infect white people," and it was "sometimes known as 'the white man's plague.'" The sequence concludes with the Man in Blue Suit continuing his lecture, framed by the vintage classroom environment, as the text overlay remains on screen, underscoring the video’s critical examination of historical medical racism, all set against the persistent, somber background music.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with the Man in Blue Suit, a male lecturer with a clear, articulate voice, standing behind a wooden desk in a vintage classroom setting. He delivers a lecture, gesturing expressively, discussing the 'aspect of colonialism itself' and how, toward the end of the 19th century, 'the romanticization of consumption began to decline' as illness was no longer seen as exclusively a disease of the 'rich and brilliant.' He explains that scholars shifted their focus from 'languorous, fainting young women and their romantic lovers' to 'the miserable humanity living in the dreary tenements born of the industrial revolution.' As he speaks of 'tentacular cities' where 'men, women, and children, pale too, often cold and starving, working long hours in dark and crowded shops, breathing smoke and coal dust,' the scene cuts to a series of grainy, blue-tinted archival clips depicting intimate embraces, bustling street scenes, city crowds, industrial workers around machinery, and a crowded room with people at desks, visually illustrating the harsh realities of early 20th-century urban and industrial life. The Man in Blue Suit then states that 'Tuberculosis was there, breeding suffering and misery, without romance.' The video returns to him briefly before transitioning to a triptych of medical imagery, featuring sepia-toned portraits of a bearded man (likely Robert Koch) flanking a central microscopic view of rod-shaped bacteria labeled 'm. tuberculosis' with the year '1882,' emphasizing the historical discovery of the tuberculosis bacterium. He concludes by noting that 'arguments raged over what caused the disease.'

The speaker, identified as the Man in Blue Suit, continues his lecture in a vintage classroom setting, standing behind a wooden desk with a chalkboard displaying medical notes from January 26, 1909, including 'Toxic Insanities: alcohol and various drugs' and 'Pathology of Gross Lesions.' He states that 'just as Britain was ground zero for the industrial revolution, it was ground zero for the rise of tuberculosis,' and that 'TB grow with industrialization in other communities as well, from India to Nigeria.' The visual then shifts to a rural wooden house where a woman interviews seated individuals on a porch, including a woman with a dog, followed by a grainy, blue-tinted archival shot of a man on a porch with a 'Tuberculosis Prevention' poster in the background. The speaker elaborates on 'TB's parallel journey with capital,' having been 'seen in outbreak after outbreak,' noting that 'industrialization wasn't seen as the culprit' at the time. He explains that as understandings of TB shifted, 'racialized stigma did a 180,' moving from black and brown people being 'incapable of getting such a beautiful disease' to 'now, they were blamed for it.' The final frames show a man conversing with seated individuals on a porch, accompanied by a text overlay quoting Dr. William C. Woodward: 'The negro race suffers from tuberculosis, tainting the country,' which the speaker directly quotes, reinforcing the disturbing racialized medical narratives.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with the Man in Blue Suit, speaking with a clear, authoritative voice, lecturing from behind a wooden desk in a dimly lit vintage classroom. A chalkboard behind him displays medical notes from January 26, 1909, including 'Toxic Insanities' and 'Pathology of Gross Lesions.' As the camera zooms in on his serious expression and blue-rimmed glasses, he explains how "racialized medicine" initially linked high consumption rates among white people to white superiority, then shifted to attribute high consumption rates among Black people to the same concept. He emphatically states, "none of that was true," asserting that Black people's susceptibility to tuberculosis was "because of racism," not inherent racial factors. This point is underscored by a black-and-white historical photograph of a man writing at a desk, followed by a Text Overlay quoting, 'the negro is to blame for his own susceptibility to tuberculosis,' attributed to W. Michael Byrd and Linda A. Clayton. A split-screen then appears, showing a classroom on the left and a nurse with patients in a hospital on the right, as he elaborates that TB "especially preys on those who are marginalized... because they are marginalized." He highlights that this "racialized medicine was challenged," calling it "obvious hogwash from the beginning," and noting "loud rejection, especially from Black healthcare workers." The sequence culminates with a critical Text Overlay, 'smacked more of the cheap politician seeking notoriety and office by playing to passion and prejudice than a doctor discussing, philosophically, a scientific subject for the diffusion of knowledge,' from the same book, overlaid on archival images of a lab with test tubes and people boarding a mobile medical unit, as the lecturer recounts a Black physician's powerful response to such racist medical claims.
segments.visual_original_caption: The video opens with the Man in Blue Suit standing behind a wooden desk in a vintage classroom setting, delivering a lecture with expressive hand gestures. The chalkboard behind him displays medical notes from January 26, 1909, including topics like 'Toxic Insanities: alcohol and various drugs' and 'Pathology of Gross Lesions,' with listed conditions such as Anemia, Hemorrhage, Brain Tumor, and Syphilis. The setting is dimly lit, evoking an early 20th-century academic atmosphere, with vintage medical bottles and equipment visible on the side. The scene then transitions to a series of black-and-white historical photographs and text overlays. First, a photograph shows a man in a white coat writing at a desk, followed by a text overlay quoting, 'the negro is to blame for his own susceptibility to tuberculosis,' attributed to W. This is followed by a split-screen showing a classroom scene with students and a teacher on the left, and a nurse interacting with patients in a hospital setting on the right. The final frame displays a critical quote: 'smacked more of the cheap politician seeking notoriety and office by playing to passion and prejudice than a doctor discussing, philosophically, a scientific subject for the diffusion of knowledge,' also from the same book, overlaid on two archival images—one of a lab with test tubes and another of people boarding a mobile medical unit. The sequence juxtaposes the lecturer’s academic tone with historical racialized medical discourse, highlighting systemic bias in public health narratives.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a text overlay quoting W. Michael Byrd and Linda A. Clayton, criticizing a 'cheap politician' for exploiting passion and prejudice rather than discussing science philosophically, a quote read aloud by the Man in Blue Suit. This text is displayed over two archival black-and-white images: one of a lab with test tubes and another of people boarding a mobile medical unit. The scene transitions to the Man in Blue Suit, the primary speaker, standing in a vintage classroom with a chalkboard behind him listing medical topics from 1909, including 'Toxic Insanities' and 'Pathology of Gross Lesions.' He speaks expressively, gesturing with his hands, explaining that bias against marginalized people and healthcare workers has proven to be one of the great facilitators of tuberculosis over the last century. The video then cuts to a colorized historical photograph of a family eating together, followed by a black-and-white close-up of a woman in a patterned dress, accompanied by a low, atmospheric, and slightly eerie musical score. The Man in Blue Suit reappears, now in a vintage laboratory filled with bottles and equipment, continuing his lecture, detailing how even after establishing tuberculosis as an infectious disease caused by a bacterium, nobody was quite sure how it spread. A title card reading 'TREATMENTS AND THE CURE' appears over an abstract, dark, kaleidoscopic background. The Man in Blue Suit continues speaking in the lab, then the video cuts to grainy black-and-white footage of a bubbling chemical reaction under a microscope, as he states, 'man got to tell himself he understand.' A text overlay appears, echoing his words: 'man got to tell himself he understand.' The scene shifts to a blue-tinted archival shot of a crowded industrial or hospital setting, followed by a black-and-white title card reading 'TUBERCULOSIS ASSOCIATION Presents,' while the speaker discusses how attention focused on places and environments that seemed to foster outbreaks, such as crowded housing tenements and dirty factories. He concludes by mentioning public health efforts like covering one's cough or sneeze with a handkerchief, as the final shot shows a nurse in a white uniform and cap, wiping her face with a cloth, with a red circle highlighting her hand and the cloth, suggesting a moment of emotional or physical strain.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of a man's face, a red circle highlighting his beard, as a male voice begins to explain how certain practices 'did lessen the risk of spreading TB,' including 'discouraging spitting in public places like trolley cars and sidewalks,' which was common in the US at the time. The Man in Blue Suit, standing in a vintage laboratory, speaks directly to the camera with expressive hand gestures, continuing to narrate how 'people were also told not to kiss babies, or kiss at all.' He adds that 'another obsession was with dirt and dust, which again changed fashion, grooming and social habits.' The scene then transitions to a split-screen of black-and-white archival footage, showing a classroom and a hospital setting, as the narrator quotes, 'There is no way of computing the number of bacteria and noxious germs that may lurk in the Amazonian jungles of a well-whiskered face.' This is followed by a sepia-toned portrait of Dr. Edwin Bowers, with the quote continuing, 'but their numbers must be legion,' attributed to him in 1916. The video returns to the Man in Blue Suit, now with a text overlay reading 'the revolt against the whisker,' as he explains how 'fear of TB germs getting caught in beards led to what Harper's Weekly called the revolt against the whisker, ushering in an era of clean shaves.' The visual shifts to grainy, blue-tinted archival footage depicting early 20th-century urban life, showing people in period attire and a man polishing a woman's shoe, while the narrator adds, 'For women, hemlines grew shorter as anxiety rose that floor-length dresses might pick up TB germs off dirty floors.' The sequence concludes with a close-up of a hand polishing a black shoe, as the narrator mentions, 'Vidyu Krishnan points out that as women's hemlines rose a few inches at the beginning of the 1900s…'

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with grainy, blue-tinted archival footage of a man polishing a woman’s shoe, as a male voice states that "shoes became an important feature of women's fashion." This transitions to the Man in Blue Suit, who narrates directly to the camera from a vintage laboratory, explaining that "By the early 20th century, entire industries had grown up in the US to try to treat tuberculosis." The scene cuts to black-and-white archival footage of a man writing at a desk, followed by a patient in a hospital bed with the bold white text 'YOU CAN LICK TB' superimposed, all set to a bluesy song about "the TB blues." The footage then shows a sprawling rural landscape with a large institutional building, likely a sanatorium, as the narrator explains that "Dry air, sunshine, and rest were believed to be the best available treatments, and so sanatoriums where consumptives could rest and breathe fresh air popped up around the world." A sepia-toned map of the United States appears with the text 'THE LAND OF NEW LUNGS' and a red circle highlighting the West Coast, transitioning to a network of roads, while the narrator details how "Southern California came to be known as 'The Land of New Lungs,' and in a mass migration that rivaled the Gold Rush, consumptive patients traveled west." This is followed by a vintage car driving through a wooded area, then a group of men in hats eating from metal bowls. The final frames show a split-screen: on the left, a hospital ward with patients in beds and a nurse; on the right, the exterior of a large institutional building, as the narrator concludes that "By 1920, around 10% of all people living in New Mexico were TB..." before the audio cuts off.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a split-screen of black-and-white historical photographs, showing children in white smocks in a garden with a nurse, and patients in winter attire under a tree, as the Man in Blue Suit narrates about patients living in sanatoriums where dry air and open spaces were believed to heal lungs. The scene transitions to a black-and-white archival clip titled “You Can Lick TB” from 1949, featuring a man playing an upbeat, vintage-style folk/country song with male vocals, followed by a nurse pushing a cart down a sterile corridor. The footage then shows a man in a robe by a window and a frail young man in a hospital bed, as the Man in Blue Suit continues, explaining that life in these sanatoriums was "excruciatingly dull" and the job of the "invalid" was to "lie very still." A split-screen follows, showing a dimly lit hospital room on the left and a scenic mountain view on the right, accompanied by the Man in Blue Suit quoting Elizabeth Mooney: "I do nothing all day except lie here staring at the mountains. I wish they would rearrange them a bit." This is followed by a black-and-white photograph of a "TUBERCULOSIS CAMP, ILLS" in the snow, depicting a man outside a tent. The segment concludes with the Man in Blue Suit, standing in a vintage laboratory surrounded by scientific equipment, gesturing as he speaks directly to the camera, explaining that family visits were discouraged not only due to infection risk but also because patients "had to rest" and "couldn't be excited."

segments.integrated_caption: The minute opens with the Man in Blue Suit, in a vintage laboratory filled with historical medical paraphernalia, speaking directly to the camera with expressive hand gestures. He explains that patients were often "discouraged even from reading books" and that "there was no YouTube." While his voice continues, the video transitions to a series of black-and-white archival footage clips: a large institutional building, likely a sanatorium, in a rural landscape; a man reading a newspaper in a hospital bed with a nurse; a bustling city street with a horse-drawn carriage; a snowy rural road with a vintage car; and a group of men eating from metal bowls in a communal setting. His narration discusses how "many patients did recover in sanatoriums," with "rest and adequate nutrition" being beneficial, but notes that "recovery rates don't seem to have been much higher for those taking the so-called traveling cure than for those who lived at home as invalids." The video then cuts back to the Man in Blue Suit, who continues his lecture, explaining that "separating millions of the ill from their homes did decrease the spread of the disease within families" and that combined with "better overall nutrition, safer housing, and lower rates of poverty, tuberculosis declined around the world." A sepia-toned animated world map then appears, showing glowing red veins spreading across continents, symbolizing the global reach of tuberculosis. Text overlays display '1882 - 1930 mortality from TB in the US' and '80%', as the Man in Blue Suit's voiceover confirms that "Between 1882 and 1930, overall mortality from the disease in the US fell by around 80%." The Man in Blue Suit reappears, continuing his explanation that "those improvements were not evenly distributed in the American population." He elaborates that "The declines for African Americans and Chinese Americans were much lower, and for indigenous people, there was very little decline at all." The segment concludes with a triptych of black-and-white animated medical imagery, showing a stylized cityscape with glowing medical symbols, a boy drawing a large tube, and a silhouette of a person holding a pen, suggesting themes of medical discovery and education.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment begins with a black-and-white triptych of animated and archival medical imagery, featuring a stylized human figure with internal organs and a cityscape, a boy drawing a large tube, and a silhouette examining an X-ray of lungs, establishing a medical and biological theme. A male voice immediately begins to explain that despite advancements in diagnostic and treatment tools like the stethoscope and X-ray machine, tuberculosis remained fundamentally incurable. The scene then transitions to the Man in Blue Suit, a man with short, graying hair and blue-rimmed glasses, standing in a vintage laboratory amidst wooden shelves, glass bottles, and a microscope. He speaks directly to the camera with expressive hand gestures, detailing the discovery of eight different classes of drugs between 1940 and 1965 that could kill M. tuberculosis. The video cuts to a black-and-white animated sequence, illustrating detailed human lungs and a magnified view of rod-shaped bacteria multiplying within the tissue, visually representing the disease's progression. The speaker continues, revealing the "catch": the bacteria's waxy coating made it difficult to kill, necessitating long-term drug administration which fostered resistance, and no single drug was effective alone. The scene returns to the Man in Blue Suit, who, with animated gestures, concludes his lecture by explaining that it was only in the mid-1950s that a combination treatment of three drugs was tested and approved, finally making tuberculosis curable for the first time in human history.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a series of grainy, vintage archival clips, as a male narrator begins to speak, explaining how improved treatment, case-finding, and contact tracing led to the identification and treatment of new TB cases. These visuals depict nurses gathered around a patient in a hospital, a doctor examining a document, a bustling outdoor scene with people near a mobile medical unit, a close-up of an elderly man with a white beard, a scientist in a lab handling test tubes, a man polishing a shoe, a hospital patient with the superimposed text 'YOU CAN LICK TB', a large institutional building, men eating, a city street, and a snowy rural road. The narrator continues, detailing how, despite these advancements, TB continued to kill millions annually in impoverished communities, and attempts to deliver combination therapy to colonized regions were haphazard and inconsistent, forcing many to seek whatever antibiotics they could, regardless of effectiveness. The scene then transitions to a modern, well-lit vintage laboratory where the Man in Blue Suit, with short graying hair and glasses, stands behind a wooden desk, gesturing expressively as he speaks directly to the camera, continuing his lecture. The background is filled with scholarly items like glass bottles, a microscope, and a red-bound book. The video then cuts to a sepia-toned portrait of Dr. Peter Mugyenyi, a man in a dark suit with arms crossed, set against vaccine vials, as the narrator introduces him as the Ugandan physician who, in 2000, gave a speech about the global failure to get HIV drugs into impoverished communities. The narrator then quotes Dr. Mugyenyi, whose powerful words also appear as a text overlay beside him: 'Where are the drugs? The drugs are where the disease is not. And where is the disease? The disease is where the drugs are not.' The video returns to the Man in Blue Suit, who continues his animated lecture, emphasizing that the same disparity was true for tuberculosis, with curative therapy available everywhere by the mid-1960s.

A male voice states, "except for where it was most needed." This is immediately followed by a bold text overlay, 'WHERE THE DRUGS ARE NOT,' with 'NOT' highlighted in red, appearing in elegant serif font within dark, circular ink-like frames, reinforcing the provocative theme. Subtle, atmospheric background music begins and continues throughout. The scene then cuts to the Man in Blue Suit, a scholarly figure with graying hair and blue-rimmed glasses, speaking directly to the camera with expressive hand gestures from behind a wooden desk in a vintage laboratory filled with bottles, a microscope, and a red-bound book. He explains that "in the 25 years between 1940 and 1965, eight different classes of drugs were developed to treat tuberculosis," but then emphasizes that "in the 47 years between 1965 and 2012, no new drugs were synthesized to treat tuberculosis." As he elaborates on how the profit incentive for researching new drugs "went away" when TB declined in rich countries, the video shifts to a split-screen. On the left, a woman in a blue uniform sits at a desk, appearing to be on the phone or reviewing documents, while on the right, a modern automated machine fills amber bottles with liquid, contrasting manual and industrial pharmaceutical processes. This is followed by another split-screen showing a scientist in a lab coat handling test tubes on the left, and a conveyor belt of white pills on the right, emphasizing modern pharmaceutical manufacturing. The Man in Blue Suit reappears, continuing his lecture with animated gestures, asserting that a system "driven exclusively by profit incentive is, of course, not the only way to develop drugs," and referencing Dr. Carol Mitnick regarding the failure to develop drugs for illnesses "that are not seen as prof..." The warm, consistent lighting focuses on the speaker and the vintage lab, while the split-screen segments introduce a modern, clinical contrast.

segments.integrated_caption: The minute begins with a split-screen displaying modern pharmaceutical production, showing an automated machine filling amber bottles and a woman monitoring medical imaging screens. Overlaid text quotes Dr. Carole Mitnick, which a male voice (the Man in Blue Suit) narrates: “a human-manufactured problem that needs a human solution... If medications were a public good, the burden of disease would drive the priorities of the industry, and TB treatment would be varied and plentiful.” The scene transitions to the Man in Blue Suit, speaking expressively from a vintage laboratory, continuing his lecture on how “there is nothing inevitable about living in a world where developing drugs that lengthen eyelashes is more highly rewarded than developing drugs that treat tuberculosis.” As he speaks, a sepia-toned animated world map appears, with glowing red veins symbolizing the global spread of disease, overlaid with '1882 - 1930 mortality from TB in the US' and '80%'. The video then cuts to archival black-and-white footage of a scientist using a microscope, while the narrator explains how TB death rates remained steady in much of the developing world even as they declined in rich nations. A distinct printing press sound is heard as a close-up shows a printing press producing leaflets with bold text 'AIDS' and 'DON'T DIE'. The Man in Blue Suit reappears, continuing his lecture, describing how, beginning in the early 1980s, physicians and activists sounded the alarm about an “explosion” in severe tuberculosis deaths associated with the new pandemic of HIV/AIDS, as untreated HIV lowers resistance to infection. The video concludes with a final shot of the printing press in motion, emphasizing the dissemination of public health messages.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with the Man in Blue Suit, a man with short, graying hair and glasses, seated at a wooden desk in a vintage laboratory, speaking directly to the camera with expressive hand gestures. He narrates in a serious, informative tone about how individuals are "far more likely to progress to active disease as the immune system weakens," and how "far too little was done to expand access to either TB or HIV medications" in the mid-1980s, leading to "tens of millions of deaths from the intertwining pandemics of HIV and TB." The scene transitions to grainy, vintage footage of a protest march, where demonstrators carry banners reading 'WE DEMAND AN END TO LESBIAN GAY OPPRESSION' and 'LESBIAN GAY TASK FORCE,' marching down a city street. This is followed by a solemn indoor scene of a group of people holding lit candles in a room with a crucifix, suggesting a vigil. The video returns to the Man in Blue Suit, now in a close-up, continuing his narration, explaining that between 1982 and 2005, "roughly as many people died of tuberculosis as died in World War I and World War II combined." A title card appears with the text 'A FUNDAMENTAL MISTRUST' in elegant serif font over a dark, textured background, accompanied by a brief, somber musical interlude. The scene shifts back to the Man in Blue Suit, now standing at a wooden lectern in a vintage classroom with a chalkboard displaying medical notes from 1909, including 'Pathology of Gross Lesions' and 'Toxic Insanities: alcohol and various drugs.' The final shot is a black-and-white photograph of Phumeza Tisile, a young woman wearing sunglasses and a hoodie, sitting on stone steps, with her name displayed in the lower left corner, as the Man in Blue Suit adds that she "just received a full scholarship to university."

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a grayscale X-ray of a human chest, displaying dense, cloudy areas in the lungs, overlaid with the text 'I was really struggling to breathe.' A clear, articulate male voice immediately begins, describing how it was "obvious that tuberculosis was everywhere in her lungs," leading to the patient being started on "a standard TB treatment immediately." The scene then transitions to the speaker, a Man in a Blue Suit, standing at a wooden lectern in a vintage classroom, with a chalkboard behind him displaying medical notes from 1909. He speaks expressively, explaining a "detour" to describe a protocol from the late 1970s called "Directly Observed Therapy Short-Course," which appears as a text overlay in a vintage-style font. He elaborates that this protocol, known as DOTS, "really only exists because of a lack of trust that certain patients will take their medications consistently." As he continues, a close-up of a wooden desk shows two white pill bottles, a 'Mylan' medication box, and a document with numerical data, visually reinforcing his point about medication. He explains that TB is susceptible to antibiotic resistance, worsened by "haphazard treatment," and the idea behind DOTS is to have a healthcare professional "directly observe patients taking their medicine every day." This explanation coincides with a shot of a Man in a White Lab Coat writing in a notebook. The speaker concludes by noting that while the protocol initially helped by providing "regular access to adequate supplies of appropriate antibiotics," it also required "patients to find their way to a clinic or doctor," as depicted in a final split-screen scene showing a person walking in a hallway and a busy administrative office environment.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a split-screen montage showcasing hospital environments: on the left, a person in an orange jumpsuit walks through a green-walled hallway, while on the right, a crowded waiting room is filled with patients and medical staff. This transitions to a hospital room where a man in an orange jumpsuit sits on 'BED 2', hugging his knees, as a nurse prepares supplies. Concurrently, a male voice begins to speak, immediately setting the tone for a lecture. The scene then cuts to the Man in Blue Suit, standing at a wooden lectern in a vintage classroom, with a chalkboard behind him displaying medical notes from 1909, including 'Pathology of Gross Lesions' and 'Toxic Insanities: alcohol and various drugs'. He speaks expressively, gesturing with his hands, explaining that requiring daily medication for months or inpatient stays "creates extreme challenges for many people with TB." As he speaks, a text overlay appears, quoting Dr. Jennifer Furin: 'Tuberculosis is the only disease I know where the core of therapy is based on fundamental mistrust.' He continues, discussing how "antibiotic resistance is driven by so-called patient noncompliance," which also appears as a stylized text overlay. After a brief cut to a man in a white shirt walking through a dimly lit corridor, the Man in Blue Suit returns, continuing his animated lecture. He elaborates that while many TB patients fail to complete their regimen, "the concept of compliance turns out to be really complicated when you zoom in to the level of individual patient experience." A subtle, ethereal background sound emerges as he narrates a specific case, stating, "By the time she was finally diagnosed, Fumesa could not walk to the clinic by herself. She was simply too sick. And public transport was expensive, not to mention the potential to spread the illness, did her inability..." The segment concludes with a bold red serif text overlay on a dark, textured background, reading: 'MAN GOT TO TELL HIMSELF HE UNDERSTAND', serving as a philosophical conclusion to the medical discourse.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with the Man in Blue Suit, a man with short, graying hair and glasses, speaking directly to the camera from a dimly lit vintage classroom setting. He uses expressive hand gestures as he narrates Phumeza's medical journey, explaining how she was initially able to avoid daily treatment because South Africa allowed two weeks of take-home medication. Behind him, a chalkboard displays medical notes from January 26, 1909, including 'Toxic Insanities: alcohol and various drugs' and 'Pathology of Gross Lesions.' He continues, detailing how Phumeza's condition didn't improve, leading to her hospitalization and the discovery that her drugs were failing due to multi-drug resistant tuberculosis (MDR-TB). The scene then cuts to a close-up of Phumeza Tisile, a young woman in a a pink shirt and teal surgical mask, looking pensively through window blinds, with text overlays confirming her MDR-TB diagnosis. The Man in Blue Suit narrates her profound fear, quoting her directly: 'I was searching stuff online, and it was really, really scary to see because on Google so many of the people on Images were already dead. Their ribs were exposed, and I thought I was gonna be like that, too. I thought I was likely going to die.' This quote is superimposed over a chest X-ray image showing dense, cloudy areas in the lungs. The video returns to the Man in Blue Suit, who continues his lecture with animated gestures, describing Phumeza's subsequent treatment regimen involving painful injections and dozens of pills taken each day.

He narrates in a calm, informative voice about injections carrying a very high risk of permanent hearing loss. He then quotes a patient, Fumeza, saying, "I just woke up, and everything was quiet," a phrase that simultaneously appears as a text overlay on a dark, textured background. The scene returns to the Man in Blue Suit, who gestures expressively, emphasizing his points, as the camera zooms in on his earnest face. He explains that Fumeza's particular strain of tuberculosis was resistant to even more drugs, making it "pre-XDR TB or pre-extensively drug-resistant tuberculosis," a term also displayed as an elegant text overlay within a dark, circular ink-like frame. He continues, detailing how the painful injections caused Fumeza permanent and total hearing loss for four years until she received a cochlear implant, and that the drug "didn't help her at all." He reflects that if Fumeza had been able to access molecular testing or if her TB had been correctly identified from the beginning, she could have been saved her hearing and so much suffering. Instead, she was in treatment for tuberculosis for three years and eight months, during which time she took between 20... The warm, dim lighting consistently enhances the historical and academic ambiance of his lecture.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with the Man in Blue Suit, a man with short, graying hair, wearing a blue suit, white shirt, and blue-rimmed glasses, speaking directly to the camera from a wooden lectern in a dimly lit vintage classroom. Behind him, a chalkboard displays medical notes from 1909, including 'Toxic Insanities: alcohol and various drugs' and 'Pathology of Gross Lesions.' He speaks with a clear, articulate male voice, continuing a narrative about a patient's arduous treatment involving "30,000 pills" before being cured. He explains that this difficult reality persists for many patients in 2024, despite new treatment protocols that can cure drug-resistant TB in "6 to 9 months with 5 to 7 pills per day." He notes that these improved treatments are only "beginning to reach those who need them most," but access is increasing, "albeit belatedly." The scene then transitions to a dynamic split-screen montage, illustrating the impact of this struggle. On the left, a young Black woman, identified by the narrator as "Fumeza," speaks passionately into a microphone at an outdoor protest, while on the right, a crowd holds a banner reading 'PEOPLE OVER PROFIT #PHARMAGATE.' The montage continues, showing Fumeza at the United Nations, posing with others, and later holding a white spiral-bound document titled 'SIGNATURES: A DRUG RESISTANT TB MANIFESTO,' which she presents to a man in a suit. Her passionate female voice is heard, echoing the on-screen subtitles as she pleads, "I ask you to act now. I ask you for change. Thank you." The video cuts back to the Man in Blue Suit in the vintage classroom, who resumes his lecture, gesturing expressively. He continues to speak, introducing "Fumeza" as a "college graduate, a sociologist, and a leading voice in the fight against tuberculosis," whose efforts have increased access to life-saving drugs. He then discusses "Bedaquiline," a "critical medication for treating drug-resistant tuberculosis," first released in rich countries in 2013.
