segments.integrated_caption: The minute opens with the Man in Blue Suit lecturing from a wooden lectern in a vintage classroom, explaining how drug prices were artificially high due to a lack of competition. He details a lawsuit filed by Fumeza and Nandita Venkatesan, with support from the organization 'Doctors Without Borders' (visually represented by a glass panel displaying the 'MEDECINS SANS FRONTIERES CHARTRE'), against Johnson & Johnson to prevent the extension of a drug patent past its 2023 expiration date. As he speaks, a person in a red shirt and white apron organizes medical supplies, and the Man in Blue Suit continues, announcing the success of the lawsuit, which allowed for generic competition and made the critical drug 'bedaquiline' far less expensive. The scene transitions to a large white 'MEDECINS SANS FRONTIERES' ship where people are boarding via a ramp, and a medical professional in a white coat and mask is seen interacting with patients, illustrating the humanitarian efforts. The lecturer emphasizes that this outcome will enable over 50,000 people with MDR-TB to receive treatment annually who otherwise would have had little chance of being cured. The segment concludes with the Man in Blue Suit highlighting Fumeza's work in expanding access to diagnosis and treatment, and her own survival due to 'activism and innovation,' as a young Black woman is shown sitting on a park bench, reading a 'SIGNATURES: A DRUG RESISTANT TB MANIFESTO' document, visually connecting to the public health advocacy discussed.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with the Man in Blue Suit, a man with short, graying hair and blue-rimmed glasses, delivering a lecture with expressive hand gestures from a wooden lectern in a dimly lit Vintage Classroom Setting. Behind him, a chalkboard displays historical medical notes from January 26, 1909, detailing 'Toxic Insanities' and 'Pathology of Gross Lesions.' The Man in Blue Suit's clear, articulate voice explains that until quite recently, the World Health Organization's recommendations for drug-resistant TB were "supportive care," which Dr. Carol Mitnick summarized as "put people in a hut by the side of the road and wait for them to die." This exact quote appears as a Text Overlay on screen. The scene transitions to a split-screen: the Man in Blue Suit continues speaking in the top right, while the top left shows a bustling City Street Night Scene with a large digital screen displaying fluctuating numbers. The bottom panel features an Automated Pharmaceutical Production Line with clear bottles moving along a conveyor belt. He continues, detailing how in the late 1990s, Partners in Health proved that similar cure rates for MDRTB could be achieved in poor communities as in the world's finest hospitals. The video cuts back to a full-screen view of the Man in Blue Suit, who emphasizes that many effective drugs for highly resistant tuberculosis remain "very expensive," not due to their components, but because "prices are kept artificially high by pharmaceutical companies." As the split-screen returns, the Text Overlay 'antibiotic resistance' fades in over the pharmaceutical production line, aligning with his concluding point that making these drugs less rare could lead to "further antibiotic resistance." The overall atmosphere is academic and urgent, blending historical context with modern public health concerns, driven by the speaker's compelling narrative.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of the Man in Blue Suit, a man with short, graying hair, wearing a blue suit, white shirt, and blue-rimmed glasses, speaking directly to the camera with expressive hand gestures. He stands at a wooden lectern in a vintage classroom setting, where a chalkboard behind him displays medical notes from January 26, 1909, covering 'Pathology of Gross Lesions' and 'Toxic Insanities: alcohol and various drugs,' with illustrative cases like Anemia, Hemorrhage, Brain Tumor, and Syphilis, alongside the names 'Professor Sterne' and 'Dr. Jackson.' The warm, academic lighting highlights the historical atmosphere. The man's voice is clear and earnest as he discusses the fear of antibiotic resistance, emphasizing that the terrifying prospect of a world unable to treat bacterial infections shouldn't lead to reserving powerful and efficacious drugs for the rich. He passionately states he would not accept his own child being denied the best available treatment for TB, questioning how he could ask 'Fumeza's family' to accept such a world. As he speaks, the camera alternates between close-ups of his face and wider shots. A gentle, slightly melancholic musical interlude with a subtle chime sound accompanies a visual transition to a dark, textured background displaying the bold text 'THE WORLD WE CHOOSE' in gold and red. The scene then returns to the Man in Blue Suit, who continues his lecture with animated gestures. He recounts asking a tuberculosis doctor, K.J. Sung, about the 1.5 million people expected to die of TB this year, and how many would survive if they had access to the kind of healthcare afforded to the wealthy in the UK, Japan, or the US. He concludes by noting that while TB is often curable, it remains a difficult disease to treat, especially in cases of extensive drug resistance. The segment ends with a beige, patterned card posing the critical public health inquiry: 'How many would die if everyone could access good healthcare?' in elegant serif font, framed by a subtle floral design.

segments.integrated_caption: The minute opens with text overlays on a vintage-style beige card, posing the question, 'How many would die if everyone could access good healthcare?' A male voice, identified as Dr. KJ Seung, immediately answers with a bold 'None. Zero. Zero people should die of TB.' The scene transitions to a close-up of the Man in Blue Suit, who is the speaker, delivering a passionate lecture directly to the camera with expressive gestures from a wooden lectern in a vintage classroom, complete with a chalkboard displaying medical notes from 1909. He emphasizes that 'we could choose to live in a world where no one dies of TB,' acknowledging that this choice would require 'long-term, large-scale investments by rich countries to strengthen the healthcare systems of impoverished countries.' As he speaks, the visuals shift to a sepia-toned map highlighting Sierra Leone, followed by a street scene in a rural area with people and a white delivery truck, illustrating the context of impoverished regions. The camera then moves into a building marked 'OLD TB WARD,' revealing a computer monitor displaying a chest X-ray with visible lung damage, underscoring the disease's impact. The speaker continues, detailing the need for 'training and employment of far more healthcare workers and likely investments in new treatments.' The visuals show him sitting under a tree with local community members, followed by a group photo of individuals wearing orange 'Partners In Health' shirts, symbolizing collaborative efforts. Returning to his lecture, the Man in Blue Suit concludes that TB in the 21st century 'is not really caused by a bacteria that we know how to kill.' The video then displays a black-and-white historical photograph of a man with round glasses and a mustache, whom the speaker identifies as 'Dr. A. Wilberforce Williams,' before fading to black.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a split-screen, displaying a black-and-white historical photograph of Dr. A. Wilberforce Williams, a bald man with round glasses and a mustache, on the left. On the right, a quote in elegant serif font lists 'poverty, bad housing, bad sanitation, bad working conditions, long hours, high rent, [and] poor food.' A male voice, belonging to the Man in Blue Suit, immediately begins speaking, stating that 'a century ago, the real causes of TB are poverty, bad housing, bad sanitation, bad working conditions, long hours, high rent, and poor food,' directly referencing the on-screen text. The scene then transitions to the Man in Blue Suit, with short, graying hair and blue-rimmed glasses, standing at a wooden lectern in a dimly lit vintage classroom. Behind him, a chalkboard displays medical notes from January 26, 1909, including 'Pathology of Gross Lesions' and 'Toxic Insanities: alcohol and various drugs.' He speaks with expressive hand gestures, emphasizing his points as he continues, 'To put it another way, in the 21st century, the real cause of contemporary tuberculosis is, for lack of a better term, us. And that's bad news. But it is also good news.' The video cuts to a montage of historical artworks and photographs, including a medieval manuscript illustration, a portrait of a woman in a green dress, and a painting of a crowded hospital ward, while the Man in Blue Suit's voice continues, explaining that 'in 1800, there was nothing anyone could do to stop people from dying of TB.' The scene returns to the Man in Blue Suit, who elaborates, 'But we no longer live in that world, thanks to the accumulation and dissemination of knowledge about the illness and how to treat it. If we are the cause of TB, we can also be the solution to TB. And that's the challenge I want to leave you with.' The final segment features a sepia-toned bar graph, visually illustrating a decline in numbers from 2.3 million in 2000 to 1.5 million in 2021, with a question mark for 2032. The Man in Blue Suit's voice aligns with the visual, stating, 'In 2000, around 2.3 million people died of tuberculosis. In 2021, 1.5 million did. In 2032, that number could decline by...' before the audio fades out.

He speaks with expressive hand gestures, emphasizing his points. segments.integrated_caption: The minute opens with a sepia-toned bar graph illustrating a decline in numbers from 2.3 million in 2000 to 1.5 million in 2021, with a question mark for 2032, as a male voice begins speaking passionately about saving millions of lives. The scene transitions to the Man in Blue Suit, standing at a wooden lectern in a vintage classroom, with a chalkboard behind him displaying medical notes from January 26, 1909, including 'Pathology of Gross Lesions' and 'Toxic Insanities: alcohol and various drugs'. He speaks directly to the camera with expressive hand gestures, emphasizing that "millions of lives can be saved" if governments invest more in research and "curative therapy." He highlights past successes, stating that "access to HIV treatment has dramatically expanded" and "malaria deaths have dropped precipitously." During his speech, the video briefly cuts to a modern-day scene showing a young Black man in an orange 'Partners In Health' t-shirt with a stethoscope, mask, and cap, standing outdoors amidst greenery, followed by a quick shot of a woman walking through a hallway. Returning to the Man in Blue Suit, he continues his animated lecture, asserting that while "treating TB is complex" and "has always been a strange disease," it "is curable." A close-up of his face emphasizes his intense delivery as he declares, "We can live in a world where no one dies of tuberculosis. We are the cause, but we can also be the cure." The scene briefly fades to black, then returns to the Man in Blue Suit, now in a split-screen effect: he continues speaking on the left, while a dark, abstract, kaleidoscopic pattern with bold red text 'TB FIGHTERS' appears on the right. Throughout this, an inspiring, dramatic background music plays. As the minute concludes, a different male voice takes over, thanking viewers for watching their "very first crash course lecture series" and inviting suggestions for future topics, with the music continuing.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with John Green, the Man in Blue Suit, speaking directly to the camera with an engaging, expressive male voice and animated hand gestures, wearing a blue suit, white shirt, and blue-rimmed glasses against a dark, textured background. He encourages viewers to comment below and then, as a red and black 'TB FIGHTERS' logo with the website 'tbfighters.org' appears on a split-screen against an abstract, kaleidoscopic pattern, he directs them to the TB Fighters community for their work in lowering treatment costs. The scene transitions to a wider shot, revealing John Green continuing his lecture in a Vintage Classroom Setting, identified by him as the Indiana Medical History Museum, with a large chalkboard behind him displaying medical notes from January 26, 1909, including 'Pathology of Gross Lesions' and 'Toxic Insanities: alcohol and various drugs.' Vintage medical equipment and bottles are visible on the desk and shelves as he emphasizes that viewer attention matters, especially for those who watch "very long videos about tuberculosis." As upbeat, instrumental background music begins to swell, the video shifts to a full-screen Credits Screen, listing production roles such as Executive Producers Hank Green and John Green, and Host/Writer John Green. John Green's voice fades as he encourages viewers to help keep "Crash Course" free by joining their community on Patreon, with the text displayed in red and white serif font on a dark, textured background, also acknowledging the Indiana Medical History Museum. The music continues as Archival Source Credits from institutions like the National Library of Medicine and the Smithsonian American Art Museum appear, followed by a transition to a bright blue background with animated colorful lines forming the white 'COMPLEXLY' logo with a blue circular 'C' icon, concluding the segment.

entity_library.Archival Footage (You Can Lick TB): Black-and-white footage from the US Veterans Administration, 1949, showing a man playing guitar, a nurse pushing a cart, and patients. This footage is accompanied by an upbeat, vintage-style folk/country song with male vocals, featuring lyrics about being 'blue and low down' and a 'knockout showdown.'
entity_library.Historical Medical Photographs (Tubercul: A black-and-white photograph labeled “TUBERCULOSIS CAMP, ILLS,” depicting a man in winter clothing sitting outside a tent in the snow, with a wooden chair nearby.
entity_library.Archival Footage (City Street Scene): A black-and-white archival shot of a city street with a horse-drawn carriage, buildings, and pedestrians, reinforcing the early 20th-century historical context.
entity_library.Archival Footage (Rural Road): Black-and-white archival footage of a snowy rural road with a vintage car driving through a wooded area, symbolizing travel and movement.
entity_library.Animated World Map: A sepia-toned animated world map with glowing red veins spreading across continents, symbolizing the global reach of tuberculosis, with text overlays indicating '1882 - 1930 mortality from TB in the US' and '80%'. This visual accompanies the Man in Blue Suit's narration about the economic drivers of drug development and the historical impact of TB.
entity_library.Dr. Peter Mugyenyi: A man in a dark suit with arms crossed, wearing glasses and a patterned tie, set against a sepia-toned backdrop with vaccine vials visible on either side. He is identified by the narrator as 'the Ugandan physician Dr. Peter Mugyenyi,' whose powerful quote about healthcare disparity – 'Where are the drugs? The drugs are where the disease is not. And where is the disease? The disease is where the drugs are not.' – is both spoken by the narrator and displayed as a text overlay beside him.
entity_library.Split-Screen Modern Pharmaceutical Produ: A split-screen showing a woman in a blue uniform at a desk on the left, and an automated machine filling amber bottles with liquid on the right, highlighting the contrast between manual and industrial pharmaceutical processes. Overlaid text quotes Dr. Carole Mitnick, which is spoken aloud by the Man in Blue Suit, emphasizing the 'human-manufactured problem' of disease treatment.
entity_library.Split-Screen Pill Production: A split-screen showing a scientist in a lab coat handling test tubes on the left and a conveyor belt of white pills on the right, emphasizing modern pharmaceutical manufacturing, appearing as the speaker continues his critique of profit-driven drug development.
entity_library.Historical Protest Footage: Grainy, vintage footage of a protest march with demonstrators carrying a banner reading 'WE DEMAND AN END TO LESBIAN GAY OPPRESSION' and 'LESBIAN GAY TASK FORCE,' marching down a city street lined with buildings and vehicles, evoking a moment of social activism.
entity_library.Vigil Scene: A solemn indoor scene of a group of people holding lit candles, standing in a room with a crucifix on the wall, suggesting a vigil or memorial service, with individuals dressed in casual and semi-formal attire.
entity_library.AIDS School Transportation Bus: A yellow school bus with 'AIDS SCHOOL TRANSPORTATION CO.' written on it, with a man in a suit and tie standing beside it, indicating a public health initiative related to HIV/AIDS education or outreach.
entity_library.Phumeza Tisile Photograph: A still photograph of a young woman in a pink shirt wearing a teal surgical mask, looking through window blinds with a pensive expression. Text overlays identify her condition as 'multidrug-resistant tuberculosis (MDR-TB)', and her fears about her condition, including searching online images of severe TB cases and fearing death, are directly quoted by the narrator.
entity_library.Robert Koch - 1882: A historical illustration of Robert Koch, a balding man with a beard, wearing a lab coat, seated at a desk with a microscope and various lab equipment, labeled 'Robert Koch - 1882'. The narrator specifically highlights Koch's diagnosis of TB in 1882 using this microscopic method.
entity_library.X-ray of Chest with Text Overlay: A grayscale X-ray image of a human chest showing dense, cloudy areas in the lungs, overlaid with a quote attributed to Phumeza Tisile, which is read aloud by the narrator: 'I was searching stuff online, and it was really, really scary to see because on Google so many of the people on Images were already dead. Their ribs were exposed, and I thought I was gonna be like that, too. I thought I was likely going to die.' This visual reinforces the severity of her condition and her expressed fears.
entity_library.Man in White Lab Coat: A man with a beard, wearing a white lab coat over a light-colored shirt, sitting at a wooden desk, writing in a notebook, with a printer and papers nearby, suggesting a clinical or research setting.
entity_library.Split-Screen Bureaucratic Office Scene: A split-screen showing, on the left, a person in a brown coat walking toward a glass door in a green-walled hallway; on the right, a man in a gray jacket sitting at a desk with a laptop, writing on paper, while a group of people, including women in headscarves, stand behind him, indicating a busy administrative or medical office environment.
entity_library.Medication and Documentation Setup: A close-up of a wooden desk with two white pill bottles, a green and white medication box labeled 'Mylan,' and a printed document with numerical data, suggesting a clinical or pharmaceutical setting.
entity_library.Protestor with Microphone: A young Black woman with braided hair, wearing a white t-shirt with red text, speaking passionately into a microphone at an outdoor protest, later seen at the United Nations holding a document titled 'SIGNATURES: A DRUG RESISTANT TB MANIFESTO' and addressing a formal audience. She has a passionate female voice, making a plea for change, and is identified by the narrator as 'Fumeza,' a college graduate, sociologist, and leading voice in the fight against tuberculosis.
entity_library.United Nations Exterior: The exterior of the United Nations building, with flags of various nations flying, serving as a significant location where the protestor, identified as Fumeza, is seen posing with two other women, one wearing a lanyard and a 'DRUG RESISTANT TB' t-shirt, highlighting her global advocacy efforts.
entity_library.Drug Resistant TB Manifesto: A white spiral-bound document titled 'SIGNATURES: A DRUG RESISTANT TB MANIFESTO,' held by the protestor, representing a collective effort (35,000 supporters, collected March 2013-May 2014) to advocate for change in the treatment and access to medication for drug-resistant tuberculosis, a topic frequently discussed by the narrator.
entity_library.MEDECINS SANS FRONTIERES CHARTRE: A glass panel displaying the 'MEDECINS SANS FRONTIERES CHARTRE' with its red logo and French text, visually representing the 'Doctors Without Borders' organization mentioned in the audio as supporting the efforts against high drug prices.
entity_library.Person in Red Shirt and White Apron: A person wearing a red long-sleeve shirt and white apron, handling medical supplies such as bottles and containers on a table, suggesting a clinical or pharmacy setting.
entity_library.MEDECINS SANS FRONTIERES Ship: A large white ship bearing the 'MEDECINS SANS FRONTIERES' logo, with people boarding via a ramp, visually embodying the 'Doctors Without Borders' organization whose support is highlighted in the audio for its role in advocating for accessible medical treatment.
entity_library.Medical Professional in White Coat: A person in a white medical coat and face mask, seated at a desk with a laptop, interacting with patients in a clinical setting.
entity_library.Young Black Woman on Bench: A young Black woman with braided hair, wearing a beige jacket over a graphic tee, sitting on a wooden park bench, reading a document titled 'SIGNATURES: A DRUG RESISTANT TB MANIFESTO,' visually representing the 'activism and innovation' and the impact of 'Fumeza's work' in expanding access to diagnosis and treatment for TB patients, as discussed in the accompanying audio.
entity_library.City Street Night Scene: A bustling urban street at night with pedestrians, illuminated by neon signs and a large digital screen displaying fluctuating numbers, possibly representing stock market data or public health metrics. This modern, high-tech visual serves as a contemporary backdrop to the lecturer's discussion on global health challenges and drug economics.
entity_library.Automated Pharmaceutical Production Line: A clean, industrial setting showing a conveyor belt with clear bottles capped with orange lids moving through an automated filling and sealing process. This imagery visually reinforces the lecturer's discussion about pharmaceutical companies, drug production, and the economic factors influencing the cost and availability of medications, particularly as the 'antibiotic resistance' text overlay appears over it, linking industrial scale to medical crisis.
entity_library.Sierra Leone Map: A sepia-toned map highlighting Sierra Leone with a red circle, transitioning to a street scene in a rural area with people walking and a white delivery truck.
entity_library.Old TB Ward: A building entrance marked 'OLD TB WARD' with barred windows, leading to a room with a computer monitor displaying a chest X-ray with visible lung damage.
entity_library.Partners In Health Team: A young Black man in an orange 'Partners In Health' t-shirt with a stethoscope around his neck, wearing a face mask and a cap, standing outdoors with greenery and flowers in the background, visually representing modern public health efforts during the speaker's discussion of successful health initiatives.
entity_library.Historical Medical Figure Photograph: A black-and-white historical photograph of Dr. A. Wilberforce Williams, a bald man with round glasses and a mustache. The quote displayed alongside his photo, detailing 'poverty, bad housing, bad sanitation, bad working conditions, long hours, high rent, [and] poor food,' is directly read and explained by the Man in Blue Suit as the 'real causes of TB a century ago.'
entity_library.Bar Graph: A sepia-toned bar graph showing a decline in numbers from 2.3 million in 2000 to 1.5 million in 2021, with a question mark for 2032, visually representing the numerical context for the speaker's opening remarks about saving lives.
entity_library.Credits Screen: A dark, textured background displaying production credits in red and white serif font, including roles such as Executive Producers, Host/Writer (John Green), and Director. John Green's voice mentions 'produced by CRASH COURSE' and encourages support on 'PATREON,' both of which are visible on this screen, with additional acknowledgments for filming location (Indiana Medical History Museum) and special thanks. Upbeat, instrumental background music begins to play during this sequence.
entity_library.Archival Source Credits: A dark, textured background listing archival sources in white serif font, including institutions like the National Library of Medicine, Smithsonian American Art Museum, and the US Department of Defense, arranged in two columns.
